3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI has made building websites faster than ever.
You can open ChatGPT, Cursor, Lovable, Bolt, v0, or another AI tool and generate a homepage in minutes. The layout may look modern. The colors may look polished. The code may even be clean.
But after the excitement fades, many AI-built websites have the same problem:
They look good, but they do not feel like the business.
That is the real issue.
The problem is not that AI is bad at building websites. The problem is that AI is usually asked to build a website before it understands the business behind it.
The common mistake
Most people start with a prompt like this:
«“Build me a modern website for a bakery.”»
AI responds with a polished design, a hero section, a few feature cards, testimonials, and a contact form.
At first glance, it looks impressive.
But if you remove the logo, that same website could probably belong to hundreds of other bakeries.
Why? Because the AI is guessing.
It is using common patterns it has seen before instead of building from the bakery’s real identity.
A simple comparison
Imagine two developers working on the same project.
Developer A
Developer A opens AI immediately and asks for a bakery website.
The result is attractive, but generic. It says things like:
- Fresh bread every day
- Quality ingredients
- Excellent customer service
Developer B
Developer B spends time learning about the bakery first.
They discover that the bakery has been family-owned for 30 years. They learn that most customers are local office workers and parents buying birthday cakes. They find out that the owner wants the bakery to feel warm, familiar, and welcoming.
Only after collecting this information do they ask AI to build the website.
The result is completely different.
The website does not just look like a bakery website. It feels like that specific bakery.
What AI is missing
AI can generate text, layouts, and ideas very quickly. But it cannot automatically know:
- The business story
- The ideal customer
- The brand voice
- The company’s goals
- The unique selling point
- The customer’s biggest concerns
If you do not provide this context, AI fills the gaps with generic assumptions.
That is why so many AI-generated websites sound the same.
The shift that changes everything
Instead of asking AI to “build a website,” ask yourself these questions first:
- What does this business actually do?
- Who is the ideal customer?
- What problem does the business solve?
- Why should someone choose this business instead of a competitor?
- What feeling should the website create?
- What action should the visitor take next?
Once you can answer these questions, AI becomes much more powerful.
It is no longer guessing.
It is building from a clear strategy.
The Website DNA approach
This is the idea behind my Website DNA™ methodology.
Before using AI, I focus on defining the business’s DNA:
- Business DNA — goals, products, services, competitors, and positioning.
- Customer DNA — ideal customer, pain points, goals, and objections.
- Brand DNA — voice, tone, story, and personality.
- Content & Design DNA — page structure, messaging, layout, and visual hierarchy.
When these pieces are clear, AI can generate websites that feel more authentic, more useful, and more connected to the real business.
The takeaway
AI does not create identity.
It creates output based on the information you give it.
If you want websites that do not look AI-generated, do not start with the prompt.
Start with the business.
That is the difference between a generic website and a website that actually represents the company behind it.
